title: When Anger Takes the Throne — The Story of Saul
reading:
Saul's anger didn't announce itself. It crept in quietly through jealousy.

When the women sang — Saul has slain his thousands, and David his ten thousands — something shifted inside him. 1 Samuel 18:8: Then Saul was very angry. And the saying displeased him. Now what more can he have but the kingdom? The one he once joyfully accepted, he now resented.

What followed was not a moment of rage — it was a slow, calculated hardening of the heart. Saul didn't immediately reach for a spear. He conspired. He tried to have David killed by the Philistines using his own daughter as bait. When that failed, his heart got harder. 1 Samuel 18:28-29 — Saul became David's enemy continually.

By 1 Samuel 20, even his own son Jonathan could not reason with him. Saul cast a spear at Jonathan — his own son — because Jonathan defended David.

Anger blinds us to the point where your own family members are no longer seen as loved ones but as enemies.

Saul's story shows us what long-term, unchecked anger does. It doesn't stay still. It grows. It clouds judgment, corrupts relationships, and hardens the heart against the very people it should protect. Saul never humbled himself. And the throne — the thing he was trying to protect — was the very thing he lost.
reflection: Is there a relationship in your life where slow-burning anger has created distance or hardness? What would it take to humble yourself in that situation?
abide: God, protect me from the slow anger — the kind that hardens my heart without me realizing it. Soften me. Keep me from becoming an enemy to those I love.
